# Before we start

<!--
## Posit Cloud

We are running this workshop on **Posit Cloud**. Everything is already installed. Open the shared project link you received and you will land in a ready-to-use RStudio environment.
--> 

The goal of this workshop is to introduce you to Bayesian statistics using Stan.  My hope is that you leave with not only knowledge, but a working software envionment for doing your own work. Please install the required packages with:


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
install.packages(c(
  "tidyverse",
  "rstan",
  "brms",
  "bayesplot",
  "tidybayes",
  "posterior",
  "palmerpenguins",
  "vegan"
), dependencies = TRUE)
```
:::


## Why Bayesian statistics for ecologists?

Have you ever found yourself with a detailed ecological dataset and a hypothesis you are excited
to test, but struggled to find a standard statistical procedure that fits?
Bayesian statistics are exciting for ecologists because they let you design a model that meets
the full complexity of your data and your ecological ideas.

Three ideas underpin everything in this workshop:

- **Probability as a language for uncertainty.** We assign probability distributions to everything
  we do not yet know: parameters, future observations, missing data.
- **Bayes' theorem** updates prior beliefs about parameters with the evidence in the data to
  produce a *posterior* distribution.
- **Generative models.** Because a Bayesian model is a complete probability model of the
  data-generating process, we can *simulate* from it. This makes model checking visual and
  intuitive.

$$
\underbrace{p(\theta \mid y)}_{\text{posterior}}
\;\propto\;
\underbrace{p(y \mid \theta)}_{\text{likelihood}}
\;\times\;
\underbrace{p(\theta)}_{\text{prior}}
$$

## The toolkit

Stan is a language and software distinct from R. It has a large a diverse community around it. I'm going to focus on two ways of interacting with it, but there are four::

| Tool | What it does | When to use |
|---|---|---|
| **Stan / rstan** | Writes, compiles, and samples models | Full control! You can use this to create a huge range of models|
| **brms** | Generates Stan code from an extension of lme4 formula syntax | great way to develop ideas, accessible because of the formula interface |
| **rstanarm** | pre-compiled models and a drop-in replacement for lme4 | fast, lightweight |
| **Stan / cmdstanr** | Stan code is the same as for rstan, but R interface is a little different | Developed faster than rstan, a little more efficient, a little harder to install |


The models in this workshop are made with a mix of these methods, mostly Stan and run in R via Rstan. There are also examples with brms as well. 

After the workshop, you may prefer cmdstan / CmdStanR for a more up-to-date experience.
